seoTitle: How to Create Jira Issues From Email Easily
metaDescription: Set up incoming email handling in Jira so emails automatically become tickets, with default issue types and access restrictions configured.
intro: Forcing clients or teammates to log into Jira just to report a bug adds unnecessary friction. This guide covers setting up incoming email handling so messages sent to a dedicated address automatically become Jira issues.

steps.heading: Open the email requests settings
steps.body: Go to Project Settings from the left sidebar and click Email Requests to reach the mail configuration screen.

steps.heading: Turn on the mail collection gateway
steps.body: Enable the master toggle to activate incoming email collection for the project.

steps.heading: Set up your email address
steps.body: Generate a dedicated Atlassian cloud email address, or connect your own custom corporate email domain if you have one.

steps.heading: Map default issue settings
steps.body: Choose which issue type — like Bug, Task, or Ticket — should be created by default whenever a new email comes in.

steps.heading: Restrict access and test
steps.body: Set domain or sender restrictions if needed, save your changes, then send a test email to confirm a new issue appears on your board.

faq.question: How do I create Jira issues automatically from email?
faq.answer: Go to Project Settings, open Email Requests, turn on the mail gateway, set up your email address, and configure a default issue type.

faq.question: Can I use my own company email domain for Jira email requests?
faq.answer: Yes, in addition to the generated Atlassian cloud address, you can connect a custom corporate domain.

faq.question: What issue type gets created from an incoming email?
faq.answer: You configure a default issue type, such as Bug or Task, that's applied to every new issue generated from an incoming email.

faq.question: Can I restrict who is allowed to create issues by email?
faq.answer: Yes, you can restrict incoming ticket creation to specific email domains or open it up for anyone to send requests.
